concerns.text: The plan proposes using a permutation test as a 'Gold Standard' to validate standard t-tests. However, standard permutation tests (shuffling labels) assume exchangeability under the null. In high-dimensional data with strong correlation ($ho > 0$), the features are not independent, and simple label permutation may not preserve the exact joint null distribution required to serve as a valid reference for the *marginal* t-test p-values. If the permutation test itself is biased by the correlation structure, the comparison (KS statistic) becomes invalid. The plan must explicitly define the permutation strategy (e.g., sign-flipping for one-sample tests vs. label shuffling) and justify why it preserves the specific null structure of the correlated data better than the theoretical uniform distribution.

The plan compares the empirical distribution of p-values to a 'permutation reference' using the KS statistic. However, the permutation test generates a *distribution of p-values* (one per iteration), not a single theoretical curve. The plan is ambiguous on how the 'permutation reference distribution' is constructed: is it the histogram of p-values from a single massive permutation run, or the average of 1000 permutation runs? If the reference is noisy (due to finite permutation iterations), the KS statistic will measure noise rather than the true deviation of the standard test. The methodology must specify the number of permutations per iteration and whether the reference is smoothed or bootstrapped to ensure the KS comparison is statistically sound.

The plan uses a Compound Symmetry structure ($\Sigma_{ij} = ho$) for all $i 
eq j$. While computationally convenient, this structure implies that every feature is equally correlated with every other feature. In real high-dimensional data (e.g., gene expression), correlation structures are often sparse or block-diagonal. Relying solely on compound symmetry limits the construct validity of the findings; the results may only apply to this specific artificial correlation structure. The plan should either justify why compound symmetry is sufficient for the research question or include a secondary simulation with a more realistic correlation structure (e.g., AR(1) or block-diagonal) to ensure the findings generalize.

The plan applies 'standard t-tests' to each of the $p$ features independently. In high dimensions ($p \gg n$), the sample variance estimate for a single feature can be extremely unstable, leading to inflated Type I errors even if the mean is correct. The plan does not specify if the t-test uses the standard sample variance or a regularized/shrinkage variance estimator. If standard variance is used, the deviation from uniformity might be driven by variance instability rather than the correlation structure itself. The methodology must explicitly state the variance estimator used to isolate the effect of correlation on the t-statistic's distribution.
